nodes.title: Reject unsupported selector syntax early
nodes.why: This keeps behavior predictable. If a selector uses syntax we do not support yet, we fail fast instead of returning confusing results.
nodes.alternative: Try to partially parse everything and ignore unsupported pieces.
nodes.tradeoff: Stricter input handling, but much easier to debug and reason about.

nodes.title: Normalize selector text into clean steps
nodes.why: It collapses extra spaces and gives us a clean chain like ["#main", "button.primary"] every time.
nodes.alternative: Split on a single space and keep raw segments.
nodes.tradeoff: A tiny preprocessing step, in exchange for fewer edge-case bugs.

nodes.title: Check current node against the last selector step first
nodes.why: For a chain like A B C, the current node must be C first. If that fails, we can skip ancestor work immediately.
nodes.alternative: Scan ancestors first and validate current node later.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple early exit that saves work on most non-matching nodes.

nodes.title: Match previous steps by scanning ancestors backward
nodes.why: This directly models descendant matching: find the nearest valid parent for each required step while walking up the tree.
nodes.tradeoff: Iterative code is a bit denser, but it is fast and avoids extra allocations.

nodes.title: Use DFS with an ancestor stack
nodes.why: The stack gives us parent context without needing parent pointers on each node.
nodes.alternative: Add parentNode links everywhere before traversal.
nodes.tradeoff: Slight stack bookkeeping, but it stays DOM-less and interview friendly.

nodes.title: Collect matches during traversal in document order
nodes.why: Because DFS visits nodes left-to-right, pushing during visit naturally preserves document order.
nodes.alternative: Store unsorted matches and sort later.
nodes.tradeoff: No extra sort step, but correctness depends on traversal order staying consistent.
